EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms in an extremly moisture rich environment led to flash flooding across portions of northeast Kansas the evening of September 6th.  Several highways were closed due to water over roadways. Rain began falling around 5pm CST, and continued through approximately midnight. Flash flooding began to be reported after 9pm CST.  Doppler radar estimated that 3 to 4 inches of rain had fallen at some locations across Marshall and Nemaha Counties at that time, and 1 to 2 inches in Ottawa County.  Spotters reported 4 to 5 inches of rain had fallen between 6pm and 10pm CST in Nemaha County, and up to 6 inches in Marshall County (5.42 at Marysville at 10:25pm CST). A measured 7.05 was reported to have fallen at Baileyville in Nemaha County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hwy 71 was closed due to flooding between Hwy 63 and the town of Bern.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed across central and east central Kansas during the early morning hours of September 27. Convection during the evening of the 26th provided sufficient low level moisture for fog development, with dewpoint temperatures overnight hovering in the upper 50s to lower 60s across the region. High pressure built over Kansas as the convection pushed eastward, which allowed for calm winds through much of the night.  Adverse driving conditions reported by local law enforcement officials did not result in any accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fog initially overspread much of the county by 2am, and reduced visibilites to around 1 mile at 1am CST.  The fog thickened over the course of the next hour, and visibilites dropped to 1/4mi, and periodically less than 1/4mi between 2am and 9am CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flood waters swept a vehicle downstream, and into a creek at Fort Riley. Between 2 and 3 inches of rain was reported to have fallen across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface warm front lifted across the eastern half of Kansas overnight, as a strong low level jet drove moisture northward and over the front, which fed into elevated thunderstorms that had congealed into an MCS early in the morning of the 17th. Small hail was reported with several of the storms, but only 1 report of nickel sized hail was received. This convective system drenched the area with rainfall as well. The attendant dryline and cold front associated with the low traversed northeast Kansas later in the afternoon.  Already saturated ground across the area received another round of precipitation, which provoked flash flooding in portions of Lyon, Douglas, and Osage Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported on HWY 77 near the Geary/Morris county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered convection developed during the late afternoon of October 12th in the vicinity of a nearly stationary warm front bisecting northern and southern Kansas. Most of the storms that developed didn't reach severe limits, but one in Ottawa County during the afternoon was able to drop golfball sized hail on the residents of Minneapolis.  Later in the evening, nickle sized hail and heavy rain fell in Alta Vista.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail covered the ground in Minneapolis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed just south of the KS/NE line during the early evening hours of September 6th in the vicinity of a surface warm front. The associated surface low over the northwest corner of the state tracked slowly on a east to northeasterly path. Thunderstorms also developed along a cold front stretching southwestward from the low. Large hail and strong winds were the primary threat with the storms that passed across the Topeka CWA, but one funnel cloud report was relayed from a local Emergency Manager. A strong low level jet helped to sustain the thunderstorms through much of the night across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power pole reported down along N. 11th St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper air system anchored over the area for several days late in the month brought excessive rain with flash flooding to much of Osage, Franklin, Coffey and Anderson counties. Some parts of Douglas and Lyon counties did not escape the rain either with some flooding and flash flooding in those counties too. The highest amounts were centered in Anderson county with 15 to 18 inches of rain common. The highest amount was 20 inches in central Anderson county. 10 to 15 inches was common across much of Coffey, Franklin and Osage  counties. Parts of Douglas and Lyon counties topped out in the 4 to 8 inch range. Record flooding occurred along the Pottawatomie Creek at both Scipio and Lane Kansas...and at Quenemo and Pomona along the Marais des Cygnes. The flooding near Scipio appeared to be the worst since the 1850's. The flood gates in Ottawa were closed to protect the city from flooding along the Marais des Cygnes although the crest was not a record there. The bridge that connects the north and south parts of Ottawa was covered with water and only the flood gates kept downtown Ottawa from flooding. 40 to 50 homes were evacuated in Franklin county with 10 to 15 alone in the small community of Lane. Several swift water rescues were done in Anderson and Coffey counties...some with the assistance of a black hawk helicopter. By late Saturday, the 30th, a state of local disaster had been declared in Coffey and Anderson counties. As the month ended, a number of counties were awaiting state and federal declarations of emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hwy 68 from Hwy 268 to Hwy 75 was flooded. At least 6 inches of fast flowing water reprted over Stubbs road. Fast flowing water also over several highways in the Quenemo area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system deepened across extreme east central Kansas. 1 to 3 inches of snow was reported across Johnson, Miami, and Linn counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snow, reported across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to three inches of snow fell across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated thunderstorms developed on the north side of an east-west aligned warm front extending across central and southern Kansas overnight October 12th through the morning hours of the 13th. The intense low level jet helped to sustain convective activity through the night, and some of the more intense storms were able to pulse up and produce severe hail up to the size of golf balls. No damage was reported.

An upper level low pressure system slid across the Central Plains Friday, the afternoon of the 13th, through Saturday, the morning of the 14th. Rain fell throughout the day Friday across Northeast Kansas. Up to 3 inches of rain was reported to have fallen, before snow and thundersnow mixed in with the rain during the late evening hours Friday, primarily south of Interstate 70. Local snowfall totals ranged from a trace across Shawnee, Douglas, Wabaunsee, and Geary counties, up to 6 inches in Coffey County. Slushy, snow covered roads and heavy rainfall caused numerous traffic accidents and localized power outages across the region over the course of the event.  Minor tree damage was also noted in Franklin county, where gusty winds combined with the heavy rain and snow to weaken and break limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to four inches of snow was reported to have fallen across the county.

A number of accidents occurred due to the hazardous conditions. A few schools were also closed. Some locally heavier amounts of snow did occur...|mainly to the west and north of Manhattan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of snow was reported to have fallen in the city of Detroit. Roads were snow covered and slushy. Gusty winds were causing blowing and drifting of the falling snow.

Gradient winds sustained at 25 mph with gusts to 40 mph caused damage to four inflatable carnival rides in Randolph, KS.  Three children in one of the larger inflatable rides were blown around inside the ride as a strong wind gust flipped the inflatable ride over.  One of the children reported some minor injuries and was taken to a hospital.  The ride with the children inside flipped up against a fence and hit a power line near the fence.  The power line did not injure any children, but was snapped by the impact of the ride.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front tracked across eastern Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours of September 30th.  Strong southwesterly sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 50 mph ahead of the main trough knocked down weak branches in Ottawa County.  Cloud cover during the morning hours prevented widespread and robust convection from developing as the cold front surged across the region.  But, sufficient heating and favorable vertical wind profiles allowed for a broken line of thunderstorms to develop right around the noon hour CST.  Thunderstorm wind gusts ravaged the far east central portion of the state, knocking down tree branches and power lines during the evening.  Small hail was also reported with some of the more intense storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained 40mph winds reported ahead of the storm, with gusts up to 60mph noted during the storm's passage. Live 1 inch diameter branches down and blown around by the wind.  Pea sized hail also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed across Coffey and Anderson Counties in east central Kansas during the early morning hours of September 9th. Moisture pooled and winds sharply dropped off ahead of a weak cold frontal boundary, which helped the dense fog to develop. Once the cold front passed through the region, winds picked up out of the north and mixing helped lift the fog.  Local law enforcement and storm spotters reported adverse driving conditions when visibilities were at their lowest. Fortunately, no accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was reported across the county by local law enforcement. Visibilites were generally in the 1/4 to 1/2 mile range through the duration of the event, but periodically dropped down below 1/4 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed across central and east central Kansas during the early morning hours of September 27. Convection during the evening of the 26th provided sufficient low level moisture for fog development, with dewpoint temperatures overnight hovering in the upper 50s to lower 60s across the region. High pressure built over Kansas as the convection pushed eastward, which allowed for calm winds through much of the night.  Adverse driving conditions reported by local law enforcement officials did not result in any accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fog initially overspread much of the county by 2am, and reduced visibilites to around 1 mile at 1am CST.  The fog thickened over the course of the next hour, and visibilites dropped to 1/4mi, and periodically less than 1/4mi between 2am and 9am CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed just south of the KS/NE line during the early evening hours of September 6th in the vicinity of a surface warm front. The associated surface low over the northwest corner of the state tracked slowly on a east to northeasterly path. Thunderstorms also developed along a cold front stretching southwestward from the low. Large hail and strong winds were the primary threat with the storms that passed across the Topeka CWA, but one funnel cloud report was relayed from a local Emergency Manager. A strong low level jet helped to sustain the thunderstorms through much of the night across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated 60mph winds at the intersection of 14th and Frontier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated thunderstorms developed on the north side of an east-west aligned warm front extending across central and southern Kansas overnight October 12th through the morning hours of the 13th. The intense low level jet helped to sustain convective activity through the night, and some of the more intense storms were able to pulse up and produce severe hail up to the size of golf balls. No damage was reported.

A persistent upper air system anchored over the area for several days late in the month brought excessive rain with flash flooding to much of Osage, Franklin, Coffey and Anderson counties. Some parts of Douglas and Lyon counties did not escape the rain either with some flooding and flash flooding in those counties too. The highest amounts were centered in Anderson county with 15 to 18 inches of rain common. The highest amount was 20 inches in central Anderson county. 10 to 15 inches was common across much of Coffey, Franklin and Osage  counties. Parts of Douglas and Lyon counties topped out in the 4 to 8 inch range. Record flooding occurred along the Pottawatomie Creek at both Scipio and Lane Kansas...and at Quenemo and Pomona along the Marais des Cygnes. The flooding near Scipio appeared to be the worst since the 1850's. The flood gates in Ottawa were closed to protect the city from flooding along the Marais des Cygnes although the crest was not a record there. The bridge that connects the north and south parts of Ottawa was covered with water and only the flood gates kept downtown Ottawa from flooding. 40 to 50 homes were evacuated in Franklin county with 10 to 15 alone in the small community of Lane. Several swift water rescues were done in Anderson and Coffey counties...some with the assistance of a black hawk helicopter. By late Saturday, the 30th, a state of local disaster had been declared in Coffey and Anderson counties. As the month ended, a number of counties were awaiting state and federal declarations of emergency.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fast flowing water crossing the road at Vermont and Douglas. Depth unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog developed across Coffey and Anderson Counties in east central Kansas during the early morning hours of September 9th. Moisture pooled and winds sharply dropped off ahead of a weak cold frontal boundary, which helped the dense fog to develop. Once the cold front passed through the region, winds picked up out of the north and mixing helped lift the fog.  Local law enforcement and storm spotters reported adverse driving conditions when visibilities were at their lowest. Fortunately, no accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dense fog was reported across the county by local law enforcement. Visibilites were generally in the 1/4 to 1/2 mile range through the duration of the event, but periodically dropped down below 1/4 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface warm front lifted across the eastern half of Kansas overnight, as a strong low level jet drove moisture northward and over the front, which fed into elevated thunderstorms that had congealed into an MCS early in the morning of the 17th. Small hail was reported with several of the storms, but only 1 report of nickel sized hail was received. This convective system drenched the area with rainfall as well. The attendant dryline and cold front associated with the low traversed northeast Kansas later in the afternoon.  Already saturated ground across the area received another round of precipitation, which provoked flash flooding in portions of Lyon, Douglas, and Osage Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported to be flowing over Berryton Rd. east of Lyndon.  Water was also reported to be flowing over Shawnee Heights Rd. south of Overbrook at 165th and 173rd. A storm spotter in Quenemo reported 1.75 of rain, while another reported 3.5 in Scranton.

A strong cold front tracked across eastern Kansas during the afternoon and evening hours of September 30th.  Strong southwesterly sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph with gusts up to 50 mph ahead of the main trough knocked down weak branches in Ottawa County.  Cloud cover during the morning hours prevented widespread and robust convection from developing as the cold front surged across the region.  But, sufficient heating and favorable vertical wind profiles allowed for a broken line of thunderstorms to develop right around the noon hour CST.  Thunderstorm wind gusts ravaged the far east central portion of the state, knocking down tree branches and power lines during the evening.  Small hail was also reported with some of the more intense storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong sustained pre-frontal winds caused at least one large branch in Minneapolis to fall onto a resident's deck, driveway, and pickup truck. The branch also took out a power line. The Concordia ASOS at 11am recorded a 36mph sustained wind, with gusts up to 45mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed from east central Kansas northeastward ahead of a low over western Kansas and weak upper level shortwave that dove across east central Kansas during the late afternoon.  Strong southwesterly winds at the surface and aloft made for a unidirectional flow regime, and promoted a marginal wind gust and hail threat. Only one damaging wind report was received locally, as storms formed over the far eastern portion of the CWA, and quickly exited the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inch diameter live tree limbs down. Power knocked out at the spotter's home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rumbled across northern Kansas. A few became severe in the early evening hours. One produced a tornado in Osborne. This tornado damaged homes and two restaurants in town. Other storms in Mitchell and Jewell Counties produced hail to the size of quarters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado sat down south of Osborne and moved north into town. The twister destroyed 2 mobile homes, damaged roofs, fences, cars and windows, and damaged 2 restaurants. The Pizza Hut sustained minor damage and several employees��� car windows were blown out. Across the road, the Circle Inn lost a large part of their roof, a couple of outside walls, and substantial damage happened to the interior portion of the restaurant. A couple of semi-trailers were blown around and one ended up on top of another car. Eleven people were injured, none seriously, and were treated at the Osborne County Memorial Hospital.  The Osborne County Sheriff's office reported the most severe injury was a broken arm. (sources: Osborne County Farmer newspaper, Osborne County Sheriff's office, and the Osborne County Emergency Management Agency).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damaging wind gusts around Tonganoxie in southern Leavenworth county on 8/8/2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts to 60 mph, caused an eight inch diameter tree to be downed. At least 4 other smaller trees, also were blown over at nearly the same time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged winter storm event occurred from January 12th through January 14th. An upper level storm system moved slowly across the region at this time. Shallow arctic air in place at the surface, was overrun by moist southerly winds. A mixture of sleet and freezing rain was widespread across the area, with depths reaching from 1 to 2 inches. Several traffic accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches accumulation of freezing rain and sleet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system over the Desert Southwest, tracked across southeastern Kansas and central Missouri on January 20th and 21st. The storm left from 4 to 6 inches of snow across portions of extreme eastern Kansas. Gusty northwest winds caused blowing and drifting of the snow, with some drifts to 2 feet in height. Schools were closed and several traffic accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inches of snow, reported across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rains and an isolated report of hail, were located across east central Kansas on October 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five and one half inches of rain, measured since the event began overnight, was reported at the intersection of 95th Street and Interstate 435.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rains and an isolated report of hail, were located across east central Kansas on October 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inches of rain reported since 9 am.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three and two tenths inches of rain reported, since event began overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm deposited as much as 6.5 inches of snow in Doniphan county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six and one half inches of snow was reported near Highland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system lifted northeast into the region from Oklahoma, during the late afternoon and early evening hours on February 28th. The dynamics from this storm system provided the lift for rapid thunderstorm development along and north of a warm front by the early evening hours. There were numerous reports of hail with the storms, as well as some flash flooding. |The most significant severe thunderstorm produced a tornado in Anderson county Kansas, which then tracked east into Linn county Kansas. The EF4 tornado took a nearly 24 mile continuous path, where it caused damage to mainly rural areas. Just north of Blue Mound a single residence, garage, and several farm buildings were completely destroyed. The tornado was historic, since it was the first time anywhere in the country that a tornado was rated as high as EF4, since switching to the new Enhanced Fujita scale February 1st, 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail with a diameter of one inch was reported at the intersection of 135th and Stateline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system, which had meandered over Texas and Oklahoma for about two weeks...drifted northeast into southeastern Kansas between the 28th and 30th of June. Copius amounts of moisture and the slow movement of the system, led to tremendous rainfall totals over extreme eastern Kansas. Major flooding resulted and river levels reached near historic highs. Three day rainfall totals in Linn and Miami counties averaged around 10 inches, with isolated locations picking up nearly 15 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused 175th and Nall Road, to be closed due to flooding. High water was also reported on Highway 56.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system, which had meandered over Texas and Oklahoma for about two weeks...drifted northeast into southeastern Kansas between the 28th and 30th of June. Copius amounts of moisture and the slow movement of the system, led to tremendous rainfall totals over extreme eastern Kansas. Major flooding resulted and river levels reached near historic highs. Three day rainfall totals in Linn and Miami counties averaged around 10 inches, with isolated locations picking up nearly 15 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported between Stanley and Stilwell at 175th Street and Null Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just days after an ice storm brought one half of an inch to one and a half inches of ice accumulations to portions of northeast Kansas, another potent 500mb low deepened in the southwest United States, and lifted through the central plains Friday the 14th through Saturday the 15th of December.  Several homes in cities and rural areas across northeast Kansas were still without power from the ice storm when the snow began Friday.  Emergency shelters re-opened in Nemaha County to assist those without power.  The heavier snowfall totals were between 6 and 8 inches across Washington, Clay, and Republic counties.  Fifteen to twenty mph winds helped to reduce visibilites even more than in just the band of heaviest snowfall. Residents for the most part heeded the heavy snow warnings that were issued.  Few traffic accidents were reported, and road crews were prompt and thorough keeping major traffic arteries clear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five to sixes inches of snow was reported to have accumulated across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought a round of freezing rain and drizzle to locations mainly north of Interstate 35 early the morning of December 1st. Around one quarter of an inch of ice accumulated in portions of north central Kansas, before near surface temperatures rose above freezing after sunrise. I began to accumulate on vehicles and structures around 2am, and on roadways by around 4am.  Slick spots were reported by local law enforecement on several roads across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to one quarter of an inch of ice was reported in the Randolph area, with lesser accumulations further south in Riley County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought near-blizzard conditions to portions of northeast Kansas during the morning hours of December 22, the busy Saturday for travel before the Christmas holiday.  Heavy snowfall and fifteen to twenty-five mile per hour winds that gusted to 25 to 35 miles per hour throughout the morning and afternoon caused significant blowing and drifting of accumulated and falling snow, and helped to reduce visibilities to less than one quarter of a mile at times.  The  heaviest snowband stretched along a line from near Wichita northeastward through Topeka, up to St. Josephs, MO. The greatest snowfall totals associated with this band were between nine and eleven inches--including a new daily snowfall accumulation record measured at the National Weather Service in Topeka of nine inches.  Traffic accidents were reported across northeast Kansas, the worst of which occurred just after 1:30pm CST in Wabaunsee County.  Thirty-two cars piled up on Interstate 70 just west of Auburn, which shut down a forty mile strech of interstate in both directions for forty miles.  One person will killed in the wreck, and several injured. Two shelters were opened to assist stranded travelers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of accumulated snow was reported in the far southern part of the county, while the northern three quarters saw six to nine inches of snow, with one spotter reporting eleven inches of accumulation in the northwest corner of the county. Three to four foot snow drifts were also observed.  Thirteen accidents (twelve non-injury, and one hit-and-run) were reported around Emporia between 10:13am and 11:51pm.  Three to four foot snow drifts were also observed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of thunder and sleet, as well as thunder with a mix of sleet and snow was also reported by residents across the county.  Up to an inch of sleet fell before the precipitation type switched over to all snow. The county saw five to eight inches of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow fell across portions of  northeast Kansas during the morning and early afternoon hours of December 6th.  A thin coating of ice and sleet accumulated on elevated surfaces, vehicles, and some roadways mainly north of Interstate 70. Sites south of Interstate 70 generally saw the mixy precipitation continue through the morning hours. By the late morning, all snow was reported to be falling across the area.  When all was said and done, 2 to 4 inches of snow had accumulated  across northern portions of the county warning area. Several traffic accidents occurred as a result of the slippery roads and reduced visibilities where some of the heavier snow was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of freezing drizzle, sleet and snow created hazardous driving conditions throughout the county. Lawrence Police responded to twenty nine non-injury accidents and three injury accidents. A trained spotter reported that roadways were slick due to the mix of sleet and rain, followed by approximately 2 inches of snow.  Douglas County sheriffs responded to three non injury accidents, two of which were on Interstate 70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just days after an ice storm brought one half of an inch to one and a half inches of ice accumulations to portions of northeast Kansas, another potent 500mb low deepened in the southwest United States, and lifted through the central plains Friday the 14th through Saturday the 15th of December.  Several homes in cities and rural areas across northeast Kansas were still without power from the ice storm when the snow began Friday.  Emergency shelters re-opened in Nemaha County to assist those without power.  The heavier snowfall totals were between 6 and 8 inches across Washington, Clay, and Republic counties.  Fifteen to twenty mph winds helped to reduce visibilites even more than in just the band of heaviest snowfall. Residents for the most part heeded the heavy snow warnings that were issued.  Few traffic accidents were reported, and road crews were prompt and thorough keeping major traffic arteries clear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to seven inches of snow was reported to have accumulated across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 32 car pile-up occurred on I-70 near McFarland, just west of Auburn.  One person was killed, and several injured. Cars, a semi, and a bus carrying Air Force Personnel were involved.  A number of people were also hurt.  One of the injured was a minister, and in an interesting twist of fate, those involved in the accident took shelter at his parish.  Near white-out conditions were reported at the time of the accident.  Two shelters opened for the convenience of displaced travelers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level storm system, which had meandered over Texas and Oklahoma for about two weeks...drifted northeast into southeastern Kansas between the 28th and 30th of June. Copius amounts of moisture and the slow movement of the system, led to tremendous rainfall totals over extreme eastern Kansas. Major flooding resulted and river levels reached near historic highs. Three day rainfall totals in Linn and Miami counties averaged around 10 inches, with isolated locations picking up nearly 15 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed around La Cygne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought a round of freezing rain and drizzle to locations mainly north of Interstate 35 early the morning of December 1st. Around one quarter of an inch of ice accumulated in portions of north central Kansas, before near surface temperatures rose above freezing after sunrise. I began to accumulate on vehicles and structures around 2am, and on roadways by around 4am.  Slick spots were reported by local law enforecement on several roads across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to one quarter of an inch of ice accumulated.  Several slick spots on roadways were observed by the sheriff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of freezing rain, sleet, and snow fell across portions of  northeast Kansas during the morning and early afternoon hours of December 6th.  A thin coating of ice and sleet accumulated on elevated surfaces, vehicles, and some roadways mainly north of Interstate 70. Sites south of Interstate 70 generally saw the mixy precipitation continue through the morning hours. By the late morning, all snow was reported to be falling across the area.  When all was said and done, 2 to 4 inches of snow had accumulated  across northern portions of the county warning area. Several traffic accidents occurred as a result of the slippery roads and reduced visibilities where some of the heavier snow was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of freezing drizzle, sleet and snow created hazardous driving conditions throughout the county.  Six accidents were reported.  One person was injuried out of the six accidents, the injury accident occurred on Highway 24 when a female driver slid off the road and hit a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold airmass at the surface was overrun with warm moist air as a 500hPa shortwave slid across the central plains states. Freezing drizzle and light freezing rain began the evening of the 8th, and mixed with periods of snow during the morning hours of the 9th, north of Interstate 35. One quarter to one half of an inch of ice and sleet accumulated in Anderson and Coffey counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drizzle through the morning of the 8th accumulated a glaze of ice on roadways causing slick spots across county roads.  Temperatures just above the freezing mark during the afternoon allowed melting of the glaze. Roads re-froze Saturday, the night of the 8th, forcing road crews to go out and treat roads for the second time that day.

One quarter to one half of an inch of ice and sleet accumulated in Anderson and Coffey counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several traffic accidents reported in Topeka early the morning of the 8th.  A series of four chain-reaction accidents started around 10:15am on I-70 west of Lecompton.  Westbound lanes were closed for about an hour.  No serious injuries were reported.   Less than 10 injury accidents were reported after the noon hour.  Several events were cancelled or postponed, and for the most part people stayed home and off of the icy roadways.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Junction City police department reported 27 non-injury accidents, and one injury accident on the 8th as a result of slick roadways.  The Geary County Sheriff���s Department reported seven non-injury accident, and two injury accidents as a result of the icy roadways, most of which were on I-70 east of Junction City, while K-18, K-57, US-77, and K-244 were also affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front combined with an upper level storm system, produced widespread flash flooding and large hail across the area from late on May 5th through May 6th 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Marais Des Cygnes River at Osawatomie crested at 38.87 feet, or 10.87 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front combined with an upper level storm system, produced widespread flash flooding and large hail across the area from late on May 5th through May 6th 2007.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Missouri River at Atchison crested at 29.15 feet, or 7.15 feet above flood stage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just days after an ice storm brought one half of an inch to one and a half inches of ice accumulations to portions of northeast Kansas, another potent 500mb low deepened in the southwest United States, and lifted through the central plains Friday the 14th through Saturday the 15th of December.  Several homes in cities and rural areas across northeast Kansas were still without power from the ice storm when the snow began Friday.  Emergency shelters re-opened in Nemaha County to assist those without power.  The heavier snowfall totals were between 6 and 8 inches across Washington, Clay, and Republic counties.  Fifteen to twenty mph winds helped to reduce visibilites even more than in just the band of heaviest snowfall. Residents for the most part heeded the heavy snow warnings that were issued.  Few traffic accidents were reported, and road crews were prompt and thorough keeping major traffic arteries clear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around sixes inches of snow was reported to have accumulated across the county. Emergency shelters that had been established after the ice storm were re-opened for residents of the county that were still without power during this winter weather event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm system brought a round of freezing rain and drizzle to locations mainly north of Interstate 35 early the morning of December 1st. Around one quarter of an inch of ice accumulated in portions of north central Kansas, before near surface temperatures rose above freezing after sunrise. I began to accumulate on vehicles and structures around 2am, and on roadways by around 4am.  Slick spots were reported by local law enforecement on several roads across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to one half of an inch of ice accumulated on bridges and roads across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A devastating freezing rain event unfolded across parts of Southern and Central Kansas from December 10th through the 11th. At the same time an Arctic air mass was moving south into the Central Plains, a mid and upper level storm system was spinning in the Four Corners area. This storm system allowed for very warm and very moist conditions to override the Arctic air. The result was a very large swath of freezing rain that in some areas approached two inches thick. The weight of the ice encrusting everything, which adds nearly 2 tons of weight to a typical line span, resulted in thousands of downed power poles and tens of thousands of downed power lines. This naturally resulted in tens of thousands of Central and Southern Kansas residents losing power, some for more than a week. Some estimates place the number without power at one point was nearly 200 thousand across the state. An unknown number of trees were damaged or destroyed in the ice storm with some cities reporting more 80 percent of trees sustaining some form of damage. Damage to just the electric infrastructure will likely top $100 million. Damage to trees could add millions more to the final tally.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roughly one inch of ice encrusted Kingman County during the ice storm which resulted in approximately 1000 downed power poles and power lines down throughout the county. Damage to the electrical infrastructure is estimated at around $15 million. There was also widespread damage to trees but the cost of the damage is unknown. The Kingman Leader-Courier contributed to this report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A half to one inch of ice accumulated across northern Sedgwick County during the ice storm. This resulted in approximately 100 downed power poles and 400 downed lines. Damage to the electrical infrastructure is estimated at $1.9 million. The city of Wichita was spared the full brunt of the storm as temperatures hovered around 32 degrees all night. Several hundred to several thousand people lost power in the city though.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful area of low pressure brought heavy snow and near blizzard conditions to portions of central, south-central and southeast Kansas on December 22nd. Heavy snow began to fall across the area between 0700 and 1000 CST. The heavy snow spread east and northeast as the day progressed, before coming to an end between 1600-1800 CST. A swath of 6 to 9 inches of snow accumulated, generally along the Kansas Turnpike corridor. The heavy snow in concert with 25 to 40 mph winds reduced visibilities to less than one-quarter mile for several hours across the area, and in fact near whiteout conditions were periodically reported. Numerous traffic accidents and slide-offs occurred, and flights at the Wichita Mid-Continent Airport were either delayed or canceled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven inches was reported in Rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that passed through the central plains the 8th and 9th left behind a chilly, shallow arctic air mass across northeast Kansas.  A strong upper level storm system lifted northeastward from the desert southwest, and supplied ample large scale forcing and energy to sustain widespread lift across northeast Kansas.  Southerly mid-level wind flow allowed for substantial warm and moist air advection throughout the 10th and 11th.  During the twenty-four hour period between around noon the 10th and noon the 11th, significant ice accumulations were reported across northeast Kansas.  Counties from Morris and Dickinson northeastward to Nemaha and Brown were coated with between three quarters of an inch and one and a half inches of ice.  These counties were the hardest hit across the local area.  Surrounding sites generally saw accumulations between one quarter and three quarters of an inch thick.  Regardless, vehicle accidents were reported across the region, homes and businesses were left without power, and the structural integrity of trees, power lines, as well as some buildings and homes were severely compromised by the weight of the ice.  Ten to twenty mile per hour winds on the tail of the system exacerbated the structural problems and caused further damage, as did melting and refreezing of the ice over the next several days.  Thousands of trees were reported to be damaged or down. President Bush issued an emergency disaster declaration for the state the 12th.  Forty-five emergency shelters were opened in Clay, Washington, Dickinson, Marshall, Riley, Geary, Morris, Nemaha, Pottawatomie, Wabaunsee, Brown, Jefferson, Jackson, Shawnee, and Coffey Counties to assist those without electricity and heat to their homes--temperatures during this time frame only reached a few degrees above freezing for a brief time during the afternoon.  Schools and businesses across northeast Kansas were closed the 11th and 12th, some for several days after that as well, through the weekend of the 15th and 16th. Kansas State and Washburn Universities closed their campuses in the midst of finals week���prompting students to make alternate preparations for their exams.  Businesses across northeast Kansas were left without power.  As a result, several lost money and residents who hadn���t previously loaded up on provisions were left unable to do so.  Refrigerated sections of stores posted the biggest losses.  Electricity-wise, the morning of the 12th, Westar Energy reported seventy-six thousand customers were without power.  Seven hundred tree trimmers and three hundred linemen went to work restoring power.  More and more outages were reported as ice began to melt and stressed trees and tree limbs eventually gave out over the course of the next week.  Nearly eight-thousand Kansas residents were still without power from Westar Energy a week after the event.  Bluestem Electric reported that 60% of their customers in an eleven county area in northeast Kansas were without power as of the afternoon of the 11th.  Sixty to seventy percent of all power poles were down in Wabaunsee, Jackson, and Pottawatomie Counties.  The afternoon of the 13th, one hundred and thirty thousand residents still did not have power.   The evening of the 14th one hundred and six thousand Kansas residents were still without power.  The ice storm was projected to cost the Leavenworth-Jefferson Electric Coop nine million dollars, its��� biggest storm loss ever.  Westar Energy stated that this ice storm may go down as the largest event to task their system thus far.  Westar linemen from twenty-one other states totaling one thousand, nine hundred and fifty workers assisted Kansas crews to fix their two thousand power poles that fell.  Two hundred poles fell in Marshall County alone.  Westar repairs totaled more than forty million dollars.  Kansas Electric Cooperatives turned to more than one thousand linemen from nine other states for support to get power back on for many Kansas residents.  It was reported that mainly wooden poles and not metal poles were affected by the accumulated ice.  Broken poles will be replaced by more wooden poles.  Two-thousand, five hundred power poles were reported down by Westar, and five thousand for other cooperatives with seven thousand spans of lines down, and nine thousand disabled service lines running from transformers to homes.  Three linemen were injured during the reconnection/rebuilding process.  Two slipped on ice (broken ankle, broken elbow) and a third was hit in the face by a tool.  Three thousand, two hundred Kansans were still without power eleven days after the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Riley County:  One of the hardest hit areas, Riley County reported some of the most widespread power outages for counties in northeast Kansas.  16,000 customers in Manhattan and surrounding areas alone were without power.  For many, power periodically went up and down the 10th through 13th.  Downed trees and power lines were the greatest concerns, and roads for the most part were in good condition due to the hard work of local road crews.  Bluemont Rd was blocked from 3rd St. to Juliette Ave. due to a downed power pole.  Several streets in Manhattan were impassible because of downed limbs, including Claflin near College Ave, other portions of Claflin, as well as Yuma Ave.  Businesses, including local newspaper offices, across the county were left without power, and were unable to serve customers.  Shelters were setup in Fort Riley and Manhattan to accommodate those left without power.  Thirteen thousand Manhattan residents were still without power the morning of the 13th.  Power was returned to Ft. Riley the morning of the 17th.  Two people were injured when they came in contact with downed power lines (a 38-year-old man and his 15-year-old son).  At least 1,000 power poles were broken in the city and surrounding area alone. The evening of the 18th, 200 people still did not have power in Riley County.    At least one house fire was blamed on the weather, a $210,000 loss.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pockets of freezing drizzle or light freezing rain caused numerous accidents across South Central Kansas. Most of the precipitation occurred just prior to rush hour. This resulted in hundreds of accidents in Reno and especially Sedgwick County. There was one fatality accident reported in Reno County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Icy roads caused at least 130 accidents across Sedgwick County during the morning rush hour including 34 injury accidents. It is unclear how severe any of the injuries were. The Iola Register contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic high pressure was moving south into the Midwest the morning of December 9th which undercut warm moist conditions aloft. This setup resulted in in a prolonged period, roughly 24 hours, of freezing rain and sleet. The hardest hit areas were Montgomery and Labette Counties where at least half an inch to one inch of ice accumulated. This resulted in numerous locations losing power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 7 pm on the evening of December 8th a car traveling on U.S. 59 was struck head on by a SUV. The accident resulted in 5 injuries and one fatality. The Chanute Tribune contributed to this report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic high pressure moved into the Midwest resulting in pockets of freezing drizzle across Central Kansas. There was not a lot of accumulation of ice but roads become hazardous enough to cause one fatality accident.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle caused Interstate 70 in Russell county to become very slick. This caused a westbound driving pickup to lose control, cross the median, and collided head on with an eastbound Kenworth tractor-trailer. The vehicles crashed through KDOT barrier before coming to rest on old Highway 40 which runs parallel to I-70. The accident occurred near mile marker 197 roughly 2 miles west of Dorrance at around 7:30 AM December 8th. The driver of the pickup was killed instantly. The Russell County News contributed to this report.

A strong upper level storm system lifted northeastward from the desert southwest, and supplied ample large scale forcing and energy to sustain widespread lift across northeast Kansas.  Southerly mid-level wind flow allowed for substantial warm and moist air advection throughout the 10th and 11th.  During the twenty-four hour period between around noon the 10th and noon the 11th, significant ice accumulations were reported across northeast Kansas.  Counties from Morris and Dickinson northeastward to Nemaha and Brown were coated with between three quarters of an inch and one and a half inches of ice.  These counties were the hardest hit across the local area.  Surrounding sites generally saw accumulations between one quarter and three quarters of an inch thick.  Regardless, vehicle accidents were reported across the region, homes and businesses were left without power, and the structural integrity of trees, power lines, as well as some buildings and homes were severely compromised by the weight of the ice.  Ten to twenty mile per hour winds on the tail of the system exacerbated the structural problems and caused further damage, as did melting and refreezing of the ice over the next several days.  Thousands of trees were reported to be damaged or down. President Bush issued an emergency disaster declaration for the state the 12th.  Forty-five emergency shelters were opened in Clay, Washington, Dickinson, Marshall, Riley, Geary, Morris, Nemaha, Pottawatomie, Wabaunsee, Brown, Jefferson, Jackson, Shawnee, and Coffey Counties to assist those without electricity and heat to their homes--temperatures during this time frame only reached a few degrees above freezing for a brief time during the afternoon.  Schools and businesses across northeast Kansas were closed the 11th and 12th, some for several days after that as well, through the weekend of the 15th and 16th. Kansas State and Washburn Universities closed their campuses in the midst of finals week���prompting students to make alternate preparations for their exams.  Businesses across northeast Kansas were left without power.  As a result, several lost money and residents who hadn���t previously loaded up on provisions were left unable to do so.  Refrigerated sections of stores posted the biggest losses.  Electricity-wise, the morning of the 12th, Westar Energy reported seventy-six thousand customers were without power.  Seven hundred tree trimmers and three hundred linemen went to work restoring power.  More and more outages were reported as ice began to melt and stressed trees and tree limbs eventually gave out over the course of the next week.  Nearly eight-thousand Kansas residents were still without power from Westar Energy a week after the event.  Bluestem Electric reported that 60% of their customers in an eleven county area in northeast Kansas were without power as of the afternoon of the 11th.  Sixty to seventy percent of all power poles were down in Wabaunsee, Jackson, and Pottawatomie Counties.  The afternoon of the 13th, one hundred and thirty thousand residents still did not have power.   The evening of the 14th one hundred and six thousand Kansas residents were still without power.  The ice storm was projected to cost the Leavenworth-Jefferson Electric Coop nine million dollars, its��� biggest storm loss ever.  Westar Energy stated that this ice storm may go down as the largest event to task their system thus far.  Westar linemen from twenty-one other states totaling one thousand, nine hundred and fifty workers assisted Kansas crews to fix their two thousand power poles that fell.  Two hundred poles fell in Marshall County alone.  Westar repairs totaled more than forty million dollars.  Kansas Electric Cooperatives turned to more than one thousand linemen from nine other states for support to get power back on for many Kansas residents.  It was reported that mainly wooden poles and not metal poles were affected by the accumulated ice.  Broken poles will be replaced by more wooden poles.  Two-thousand, five hundred power poles were reported down by Westar, and five thousand for other cooperatives with seven thousand spans of lines down, and nine thousand disabled service lines running from transformers to homes.  Three linemen were injured during the reconnection/rebuilding process.  Two slipped on ice (broken ankle, broken elbow) and a third was hit in the face by a tool.  Three thousand, two hundred Kansans were still without power eleven days after the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter of an inch to one inch of ice was reported to have accumulated on surfaces across the county.

No children were on board.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic high pressure was moving south into the Midwest the morning of December 9th which undercut warm moist conditions aloft. This setup resulted in in a prolonged period, roughly 24 hours, of freezing rain and sleet. The hardest hit areas were Montgomery and Labette Counties where at least half an inch to one inch of ice accumulated. This resulted in numerous locations losing power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately one quarter of an inch of ice and sleet accumulated across Wilson county. It is unknown how much damage was caused.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pockets of freezing drizzle or light freezing rain caused numerous accidents across South Central Kansas. Most of the precipitation occurred just prior to rush hour. This resulted in hundreds of accidents in Reno and especially Sedgwick County. There was one fatality accident reported in Reno County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A motorist lost control of her vehicle on K-61 and rolled into a ditch. The driver was ejected from the vehicle and killed. The Iola Register contributed to this report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One quarter to three quarters of an inch of ice accumulated on surfaces across the county.  Nearly 80% of Junction City residents were without left without power, and 65% of the power lines in the city were down.  Shelters were set up in Junction City at Spin City, as well as the Municipal Building for local residents, as well as stranded travelers.  Around 200 people utilized the facilities the night of the 11th, despite the fact that heat was not available.  Over 70% of Geary County residents were without power the morning of the 12th, while only 10% of Chapman was without power the morning of the 12th.  80% of the Fort Riley post was without power between the 11th and 12th, much of which retuned during the afternoon of the 12th.  Employees across the county were told not to come to work for safety reasons.  Many power poles were broken, and numerous power lines were reported to have been down.  Broken tree limbs covered the ground across much of Junction City, and heavily iced branches hung over many city streets.  A total of 4500 Westar Energy customers were still without power in Geary County the morning of the 13th.  A 90-year-old woman was found dead outside her home in Junction City.  The family believes she walked outside, stumbled, and froze.

Ten to twenty mile per hour winds on the tail of the system exacerbated the structural problems and caused further damage, as did melting and refreezing of the ice over the next several days.  Thousands of trees were reported to be damaged or down. President Bush issued an emergency disaster declaration for the state the 12th.  Forty-five emergency shelters were opened in Clay, Washington, Dickinson, Marshall, Riley, Geary, Morris, Nemaha, Pottawatomie, Wabaunsee, Brown, Jefferson, Jackson, Shawnee, and Coffey Counties to assist those without electricity and heat to their homes--temperatures during this time frame only reached a few degrees above freezing for a brief time during the afternoon.  Schools and businesses across northeast Kansas were closed the 11th and 12th, some for several days after that as well, through the weekend of the 15th and 16th. Kansas State and Washburn Universities closed their campuses in the midst of finals week���prompting students to make alternate preparations for their exams.  Businesses across northeast Kansas were left without power.  As a result, several lost money and residents who hadn���t previously loaded up on provisions were left unable to do so.  Refrigerated sections of stores posted the biggest losses.  Electricity-wise, the morning of the 12th, Westar Energy reported seventy-six thousand customers were without power.  Seven hundred tree trimmers and three hundred linemen went to work restoring power.  More and more outages were reported as ice began to melt and stressed trees and tree limbs eventually gave out over the course of the next week.  Nearly eight-thousand Kansas residents were still without power from Westar Energy a week after the event.  Bluestem Electric reported that 60% of their customers in an eleven county area in northeast Kansas were without power as of the afternoon of the 11th.  Sixty to seventy percent of all power poles were down in Wabaunsee, Jackson, and Pottawatomie Counties.  The afternoon of the 13th, one hundred and thirty thousand residents still did not have power.   The evening of the 14th one hundred and six thousand Kansas residents were still without power.  The ice storm was projected to cost the Leavenworth-Jefferson Electric Coop nine million dollars, its��� biggest storm loss ever.  Westar Energy stated that this ice storm may go down as the largest event to task their system thus far.  Westar linemen from twenty-one other states totaling one thousand, nine hundred and fifty workers assisted Kansas crews to fix their two thousand power poles that fell.  Two hundred poles fell in Marshall County alone.  Westar repairs totaled more than forty million dollars.  Kansas Electric Cooperatives turned to more than one thousand linemen from nine other states for support to get power back on for many Kansas residents.  It was reported that mainly wooden poles and not metal poles were affected by the accumulated ice.  Broken poles will be replaced by more wooden poles.  Two-thousand, five hundred power poles were reported down by Westar, and five thousand for other cooperatives with seven thousand spans of lines down, and nine thousand disabled service lines running from transformers to homes.  Three linemen were injured during the reconnection/rebuilding process.  Two slipped on ice (broken ankle, broken elbow) and a third was hit in the face by a tool.  Three thousand, two hundred Kansans were still without power eleven days after the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulated ice was up to half of an inch thick was reported across the county.  The town of Miltonvale was without power sporadically the morning of the 11th, and outages were otherwise scattered across the county.  Schools, businesses, many government offices, and other events were called off across the county, and public transportation was shut down during and for 2 days following the event.  Tree limbs were reported to have fallen across several city and county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful area of low pressure brought heavy snow and near blizzard conditions to portions of central, south-central and southeast Kansas on December 22nd. Heavy snow began to fall across the area between 0700 and 1000 CST. The heavy snow spread east and northeast as the day progressed, before coming to an end between 1600-1800 CST. A swath of 6 to 9 inches of snow accumulated, generally along the Kansas Turnpike corridor. The heavy snow in concert with 25 to 40 mph winds reduced visibilities to less than one-quarter mile for several hours across the area, and in fact near whiteout conditions were periodically reported. Numerous traffic accidents and slide-offs occurred, and flights at the Wichita Mid-Continent Airport were either delayed or canceled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine inches was reported in Sedgwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A devastating freezing rain event unfolded across parts of Southern and Central Kansas from December 10th through the 11th. At the same time an Arctic air mass was moving south into the Central Plains, a mid and upper level storm system was spinning in the Four Corners area. This storm system allowed for very warm and very moist conditions to override the Arctic air. The result was a very large swath of freezing rain that in some areas approached two inches thick. The weight of the ice encrusting everything, which adds nearly 2 tons of weight to a typical line span, resulted in thousands of downed power poles and tens of thousands of downed power lines. This naturally resulted in tens of thousands of Central and Southern Kansas residents losing power, some for more than a week. Some estimates place the number without power at one point was nearly 200 thousand across the state. An unknown number of trees were damaged or destroyed in the ice storm with some cities reporting more 80 percent of trees sustaining some form of damage. Damage to just the electric infrastructure will likely top $100 million. Damage to trees could add millions more to the final tally.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of ice accumulated across Reno County during the ice storm. This resulted in approximately 2000 downed power poles and 7900 downed lines. Damage to the electrical infrastructure is estimated at $37.5 million. There was also widespread damage to trees but the cost of the damage is unknown. The Hutchinson News contributed to this report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between half of an inch and up to one and one quarter of an inch of ice accumulated on surfaces across Nemaha County, with some of the greatest totals reported in Seneca and Baileyville.  City streets and rural roads across the county were blocked as a result of downed trees, limbs, and power lines.  Officers reportedly ran out of orange cones to block off city streets���too many had downed power lines or trees.  Rows of power poles were down in a few locations. Nearly every home in the town of Oskaloosa had downed tree limbs, uprooted trees, or leaning power poles.  Ice covered trees, limbs, and power lines were reported down across the county. Businesses across northeast Kansas were left without power.  As a result, several lost money and residents who hadn���t previously loaded up on provisions were left unable to do so.  Refrigerated sections of stores posted the biggest losses.  Electricity-wise, the morning of the 12th, Westar Energy reported seventy-six thousand customers were without power.  Seven hundred tree trimmers and three hundred linemen went to work restoring power.  More and more outages were reported as ice began to melt and stressed trees and tree limbs eventually gave out over the course of the next week.  Nearly eight-thousand Kansas residents were still without power from Westar Energy a week after the event.  Bluestem Electric reported that 60% of their customers in an eleven county area in northeast Kansas were without power as of the afternoon of the 11th.  Sixty to seventy percent of all power poles were down in Wabaunsee, Jackson, and Pottawatomie Counties.  The afternoon of the 13th, one hundred and thirty thousand residents still did not have power.   The evening of the 14th one hundred and six thousand Kansas residents were still without power.  The ice storm was projected to cost the Leavenworth-Jefferson Electric Coop nine million dollars, its��� biggest storm loss ever.  Westar Energy stated that this ice storm may go down as the largest event to task their system thus far.  Westar linemen from twenty-one other states totaling one thousand, nine hundred and fifty workers assisted Kansas crews to fix their two thousand power poles that fell.  Two hundred poles fell in Marshall County alone.  Westar repairs totaled more than forty million dollars.  Kansas Electric Cooperatives turned to more than one thousand linemen from nine other states for support to get power back on for many Kansas residents.  It was reported that mainly wooden poles and not metal poles were affected by the accumulated ice.  Broken poles will be replaced by more wooden poles.  Two-thousand, five hundred power poles were reported down by Westar, and five thousand for other cooperatives with seven thousand spans of lines down, and nine thousand disabled service lines running from transformers to homes.  Three linemen were injured during the reconnection/rebuilding process.  Two slipped on ice (broken ankle, broken elbow) and a third was hit in the face by a tool.  Three thousand, two hundred Kansans were still without power eleven days after the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One half of an inch to three quarters of an inch of ice was reported to have accumulated on surfaces across the county.  Primary problems in both towns and rural areas were downed trees, limbs, and power lines���especially limbs that fell onto power lines.  Several homes in Belleville were left without power, and residents of both Scandia and Norway also reported power outages.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulation estimates ranged from around half of an inch, to three quarters of an inch across the county.  The city of Wamego reported 45 downed power lines the morning of the 11th.  Belvue, Onaga, and Olsburg were left completely without power by the time the freezing rain ended, and Westmoreland was left completely without water.  Four hundred Westmoreland residents were still without power the night after the freezing rain fell.  Local firefighters responded to several fires, caused by downed power lines and stressed transformers.  Nearly every street in Westmoreland was littered with tree branches, tree limbs, and power lines.  Shelters were set up across the county.  Two days after the freezing rain ended, 5,000 county residents were still without power including all of St. George, Onaga, Westmoreland, Olsburg, Wheaton, Emmet, and Havensville.  Tree limbs and power lines continued to fall over the course of the week after the event.  Phone outages were also reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful area of low pressure brought heavy snow and near blizzard conditions to portions of central, south-central and southeast Kansas on December 22nd. Heavy snow began to fall across the area between 0700 and 1000 CST. The heavy snow spread east and northeast as the day progressed, before coming to an end between 1600-1800 CST. A swath of 6 to 9 inches of snow accumulated, generally along the Kansas Turnpike corridor. The heavy snow in concert with 25 to 40 mph winds reduced visibilities to less than one-quarter mile for several hours across the area, and in fact near whiteout conditions were periodically reported. Numerous traffic accidents and slide-offs occurred, and flights at the Wichita Mid-Continent Airport were either delayed or canceled.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches was reported in far northwest Wichita.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A devastating freezing rain event unfolded across parts of Southern and Central Kansas from December 10th through the 11th. At the same time an Arctic air mass was moving south into the Central Plains, a mid and upper level storm system was spinning in the Four Corners area. This storm system allowed for very warm and very moist conditions to override the Arctic air. The result was a very large swath of freezing rain that in some areas approached two inches thick. The weight of the ice encrusting everything, which adds nearly 2 tons of weight to a typical line span, resulted in thousands of downed power poles and tens of thousands of downed power lines. This naturally resulted in tens of thousands of Central and Southern Kansas residents losing power, some for more than a week. Some estimates place the number without power at one point was nearly 200 thousand across the state. An unknown number of trees were damaged or destroyed in the ice storm with some cities reporting more 80 percent of trees sustaining some form of damage. Damage to just the electric infrastructure will likely top $100 million. Damage to trees could add millions more to the final tally.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quarter to a half inch of ice accumulated across parts of Cowley County during the ice storm. This resulted in several tree limbs falling onto power lines knocking power out to 1,400 Arkansas City residences and businesses for several hours. Damage is estimated at several hundred thousand dollars. The Arkansas City Traveler contributed to this report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brown County: Three quarters of an inch to one and one half inches of ice accumulated on surfaces across the county.  Thunder was also heard during the periods of heaviest freezing rain on the 11th.  Tree limbs and power poles were down across Fairview, due to the accumulation of ice.  Whole trees were uprooted in and around Hiawatha.  Power poles were snapped across the region as well.  Many streets in Hiawatha were littered with tree limbs where an estimated ����� of ice accumulated on just about everything in the city.  Homes and vehicles alike suffered tremendous damage when large branches fell on top of them.  Much of the county was left without power, beginning about 2:30am CST the 11th.  Several fires were reported in Hiawatha, at least one of which was caused by a tree limb that fell onto the electrical box of a house.  In Horton, power lines were down, as were entire trees and many of their limbs.  Power was cut off in Horton around 11:30pm the 10th, and phone, cable, and Internet were also knocked out.  Shelters were set up in Hiawatha, Morrill, Everest, and Horton.  The Brown-Atchison Electric Coop was 100% without power���no substations had any power whatsoever.  The company serves 3180 customers in northeast Kansas.  Crews took nearly two weeks to restore power to some locations.  An emergency water conservation message was put out due to the fact that electric water pumps across the county were able to be run without power.  Several accidents were reported on roads across the county���none of which were serious.  US-159 was closed along a 5 mile stretch east of Muscotah due to downed power lines.  A portion of K-7 was also closed due to downed power lines and tree limbs.  Cell phone communication was spotty for up to a week after the event. Ice was reported to be two to three inches thick along and east of Baker Road in Brown County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to two inches of ice accumulated across Ellsworth County during the ice storm. This resulted in approximately 500 downed power poles and 2000 downed lines. Damage to the electrical infrastructure is estimated at $9.8 million. There was also widespread damage to trees but the cost of the damage is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that passed through the central plains the 8th and 9th left behind a chilly, shallow arctic air mass across northeast Kansas.  A strong upper level storm system lifted northeastward from the desert southwest, and supplied ample large scale forcing and energy to sustain widespread lift across northeast Kansas.  Southerly mid-level wind flow allowed for substantial warm and moist air advection throughout the 10th and 11th.  During the twenty-four hour period between around noon the 10th and noon the 11th, significant ice accumulations were reported across northeast Kansas.  Counties from Morris and Dickinson northeastward to Nemaha and Brown were coated with between three quarters of an inch and one and a half inches of ice.  These counties were the hardest hit across the local area.  Surrounding sites generally saw accumulations between one quarter and three quarters of an inch thick.  Regardless, vehicle accidents were reported across the region, homes and businesses were left without power, and the structural integrity of trees, power lines, as well as some buildings and homes were severely compromised by the weight of the ice.  Ten to twenty mile per hour winds on the tail of the system exacerbated the structural problems and caused further damage, as did melting and refreezing of the ice over the next several days.  Thousands of trees were reported to be damaged or down. President Bush issued an emergency disaster declaration for the state the 12th.  Forty-five emergency shelters were opened in Clay, Washington, Dickinson, Marshall, Riley, Geary, Morris, Nemaha, Pottawatomie, Wabaunsee, Brown, Jefferson, Jackson, Shawnee, and Coffey Counties to assist those without electricity and heat to their homes--temperatures during this time frame only reached a few degrees above freezing for a brief time during the afternoon.  Schools and businesses across northeast Kansas were closed the 11th and 12th, some for several days after that as well, through the weekend of the 15th and 16th. Kansas State and Washburn Universities closed their campuses in the midst of finals week���prompting students to make alternate preparations for their exams.  Businesses across northeast Kansas were left without power.  As a result, several lost money and residents who hadn���t previously loaded up on provisions were left unable to do so.  Refrigerated sections of stores posted the biggest losses.  Electricity-wise, the morning of the 12th, Westar Energy reported seventy-six thousand customers were without power.  Seven hundred tree trimmers and three hundred linemen went to work restoring power.  More and more outages were reported as ice began to melt and stressed trees and tree limbs eventually gave out over the course of the next week.  Nearly eight-thousand Kansas residents were still without power from Westar Energy a week after the event.  Bluestem Electric reported that 60% of their customers in an eleven county area in northeast Kansas were without power as of the afternoon of the 11th.  Sixty to seventy percent of all power poles were down in Wabaunsee, Jackson, and Pottawatomie Counties.  The afternoon of the 13th, one hundred and thirty thousand residents still did not have power.   The evening of the 14th one hundred and six thousand Kansas residents were still without power.  The ice storm was projected to cost the Leavenworth-Jefferson Electric Coop nine million dollars, its��� biggest storm loss ever.  Westar Energy stated that this ice storm may go down as the largest event to task their system thus far.  Westar linemen from twenty-one other states totaling one thousand, nine hundred and fifty workers assisted Kansas crews to fix their two thousand power poles that fell.  Two hundred poles fell in Marshall County alone.  Westar repairs totaled more than forty million dollars.  Kansas Electric Cooperatives turned to more than one thousand linemen from nine other states for support to get power back on for many Kansas residents.  It was reported that mainly wooden poles and not metal poles were affected by the accumulated ice.  Broken poles will be replaced by more wooden poles.  Two-thousand, five hundred power poles were reported down by Westar, and five thousand for other cooperatives with seven thousand spans of lines down, and nine thousand disabled service lines running from transformers to homes.  Three linemen were injured during the reconnection/rebuilding process.  Two slipped on ice (broken ankle, broken elbow) and a third was hit in the face by a tool.  Three thousand, two hundred Kansans were still without power eleven days after the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice between half of an inch and one inch thick was reported to have accumulated across the county.  Several house fires were reported as a result of downed power lines.  Approximately 2500 residents of Clay County was left without power the night of the 10th and morning of the 11th.  Westar Energy lost 80 power poles to ice during the storm.  Businesses and schools across the county were closed for safety concerns.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm total 2.63��� of precipitation was reported at the NWS in Topeka.  Ice accumulations ranged from around half of an inch up to nearly an inch across the county.  One quarter of residents in Rossville lost power, and one hundred and fifty homes in Silver Lake lost power.  Westar Energy���s largest plant, Jeffery Energy Center, was shut down because it had no place to send power.  Four and a half days passed before the plant was operating again. Schools across the county were closed for several days due to lack of power as well as hazardous roadways.  Minor roof leaks were also reported at a few locations, including several schools across the Topeka area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving 500mb shortwave swept across the Central Plains and deposited the first measurable snow across northeast Kansas overnight November 23 through the morning hours of November 24th. The snow generally began around 9pm CST over north central Kansas and spread eastward over the course of the next hour, before ending between 5 and 6am CST across the region. Accumulated snowfall totals were generally light, and ranged from one half of an inch to one inch across portions of Jefferson, Shawnee, and Lyon Counties, to around 1 to 3 inches across counties further north and west in the Topeka Coutny Warning Area. But, this first snowfall of the season occurred the weekend after the Thanksgiving holiday, and numerous car crashes were reported across the area as a result of the slick conditions established by the wintry precipitation.  At least one stretch of I-70 in Topeka was shut down for 90 minutes to clear the scene of an accident.

A strong upper level low pressure system moved rapidly from west Texas through western Kansas Saturday evening and night, January 20th.  This brought heavy snow to much of southwest and south central Kansas by Sunday morning, January 21st.  By the time the snow tapered to flurries early Sunday morning, heavy snow covered two large areas of southwestern Kansas.  Six to nine inches of snow fell basically northwest of a line from Johnson City to Sublette to Howell to Hanston to Ash Valley.  Within this area���the largest amounts near 9 inches fell at Garden City in Finney County and also 10 miles south of Collyer in Trego County.|The second area of heavy snow blanketed mostly south central Kansas and was southeast of a line from Liberal to Dodge City to Bucklin to Larned.  Most of this area received from 5 to 8 inches of snowfall, with Ashland in Clark County and a point 3 miles north of Greensburg both reporting 8.5 inches.  Oddly enough, there was a 20 to 50 mile wide swath from Elkhart to Montezuma to Windhorst to Sanford which only received 3 to 5 inches, and this swath was situated between the other two heavier snow areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow was reported in Utica.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late Spring snow storm took aim on southwest Kansas late Thursday night, April 12th, and did not wind down until late on Friday the 13th.  The system started as light rain Thursday night, but changed from rain to snow early on the 13th.  The snow, heavy at times, was also accompanied by north to north west winds of 25 to 40 mph, which caused considerable drifting in the west part of DDC���s CWA.  Cloud to ground lightning was also common during the heaviest snow.  The highest snowfall amount reported was 13 inches at Johnson City in Stanton County, with drifts 2 feet high.  10 to 12 inches of snow fell along a southwest to east-northeast axis from the Saunders Elevator in western Stanton County through Ulysses to east of Dodge city.  Northwest Hamilton County also reported over 10 inches of snow.  6 to 10 inches of snow fell on either side of the above mentioned axis, including the areas of Scott City, Garden City, Jetmore, Larned, Hugoton and Elkhart.  2 to 4 inches of snow fell on the periphery of the heavier snow, and included the towns of Dighton, Ness City, St. John, near Kinsley, and southeastern Stevens County.  1 to 2 inches fell along the northeastern and southeastern fringes of DDC���s CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of snow fell across the northwest part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rolled across much of southwest Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inches of rain fell overnight at a location 6 miles southwest of Cairo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated large hail fell during the early morning hours before sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail did quite a bit of damage to vehicles at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although there were isolated reports of large hail the bigger event was very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 3.60 inches fell overnight.
